title: Indications, Interpretation, and Evaluation
narrationScript: An accumulation of particles is not a defect. It is not even a flaw yet. It is an indication, and what happens next is the real work of a Level Two. We move through three distinct acts. First we find the indication. Then we interpret it, deciding what caused it. Only if the cause is a real discontinuity do we evaluate it against acceptance criteria and make the accept or reject call. Keep the three acts separate in your head, because each one can fail in its own way. Start with the instrument doing the interpreting, you. Human factors decide more inspections than amperage does. Fatigue dulls detection. Expectation bias makes a part that always passes keep passing. Production pressure sets a pace your eyes cannot honestly follow. The defenses are simple. A deliberate overlapping scan pattern. Short breaks. Vision checked on schedule, near acuity especially. And when you step into a darkened booth for fluorescent work, give your eyes time to adapt before you examine, at least a minute by common practice, longer if your procedure says so. One more trap. Photochromic lenses, the kind that darken in sunlight, darken under ultraviolet light too, so they have no place in the booth. Continuity of inspection means the examination is one unbroken thread. In the continuous method you apply the bath while the current flows, and you watch the surface as indications form. Cover the part in a planned sequence with overlap between zones, and if you are interrupted mid scan, back up and rescan rather than guessing where you stopped. Viewing conditions back up your eyes. Get the surface close, commonly within arm's reach and about two feet of the eye, at a reasonable viewing angle. Keep the booth clean and dark for fluorescent work, with ambient white light held to about two footcandles. Verify ultraviolet intensity with a calibrated meter, at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter at the examination surface. For visible particles you need generous white light instead, at least one hundred footcandles at the surface. The component itself sets the context. A new forging carries manufacturing flaws, seams, laps, and bursts. A pump shaft with ten years of service is a candidate for fatigue cracks at fillets and keyways. Surface condition matters too, because coatings and roughness cost sensitivity and breed false indications. And the purpose of the test, in process, final acceptance, or overhaul, decides both the technique and the criteria you will apply. Now the triage. A false indication is not magnetic at all. Particles held by roughness, drainage patterns where the bath ran off, lint, scale, or the overall furring of over magnetization. Clean the surface and retest, and a false indication fails to repeat, or it follows the housekeeping problem rather than the metal. A nonrelevant indication is real magnetic leakage from something that is not a discontinuity. A keyway or spline corner, a drilled hole near the surface, an abrupt section change, a press fit boundary, the permeability boundary at a weld, magnetic writing from contact with another magnetized part, or the external poles of the magnetizing setup. Prove it. Correlate the indication with the drawing, demagnetize and retest where magnetic writing is suspected, and note it on the record. Whatever remains is relevant, leakage from an actual discontinuity. Then read its shape. Sharp, dense, tightly held particles mean a tight surface crack or seam. A broad, fuzzy, weakly held pattern means the reflector sits below the surface. Evaluation is the last act. The acceptance criteria come from the governing code, specification, or customer, never from your gut. Typical criteria distinguish linear from rounded indications and set size limits, sometimes allowing none at all in critical zones. Reject what the criteria reject, mark it, and route it for disposition. Remember that anything repaired comes back for reexamination with the same technique before it can be accepted. Then close out the job properly. Postclean the part, because particle residue in bearings, hydraulic passages, or under paint causes real damage. Demagnetize and verify when required. Protect bare steel from corrosion, especially after water based baths. And document everything. Part identity, procedure and revision, equipment and settings, particle batch, light readings, what you found, where you found it, and what you decided, with your name, level, and date. An examination that is not documented might as well never have happened.
imagePrompt: In a darkened inspection booth, a technician under ultraviolet light leans close to a splined pump shaft on the rails of a wet horizontal bench unit, studying a glowing green-yellow particle line beside the keyway.

sections.heading: Three Different Acts: Find, Interpret, Evaluate
sections.body:
MT vocabulary is precise, and the precision protects you.

- An **indication** is any particle accumulation that demands attention. Finding it is detection — nothing more.
- **Interpretation** is deciding what caused the indication: a real discontinuity (**relevant**), magnetic leakage from part geometry or magnetization artifacts (**nonrelevant**), or something with no magnetic cause at all (**false**).
- **Evaluation** is judging a relevant indication against the governing **acceptance criteria** to reach an accept/reject decision.

Mixing the acts causes both kinds of error. Treat every accumulation as a defect and you scrap good parts over keyway leakage and drainage streaks. Wave off every faint line as 'probably geometry' and one day the faint line is a lap. A disciplined Level II runs the sequence the same way every time: confirm the indication is real, interpret its cause, and only then reach for the criteria. The purpose of this lesson is to make that sequence — and the conditions that make it trustworthy — automatic.

sections.heading: Human Factors and Continuity of Inspection
sections.body:
The detector in this method is a human being, and human performance drifts.

- **Fatigue and pace.** Detection rates fall measurably when you are tired or rushed. Use a deliberate, overlapping scan pattern, take short breaks, and never let production pressure set scanning speed.
- **Expectation bias.** A part that 'always passes' trains you to stop looking. Inspect the thousandth part like the first.
- **Vision.** Near-vision acuity and color perception are checked on a schedule for good reason — close MT work is fine-detail work.
- **Dark adaptation.** Entering a darkened booth, pause and let your eyes adapt before examining — commonly at least one minute, longer per procedure. **Photochromic lenses are prohibited** under UV-A: they darken and rob you of the very light you need.

**Continuity of inspection** means the examination is one unbroken thread. In the continuous method, watch the surface while current flows — indications form in that moment. Follow a planned coverage sequence with overlap between zones; if interrupted, back up and rescan rather than guessing where you stopped. One inspector should carry a part through the complete examination wherever practical.

sections.heading: Viewing Conditions and Lighting
sections.body:
Viewing conditions are part of the technique, not a comfort item.

- **Distance and angle.** Get the surface close — common practice keeps the eye within about 24 inches of the surface at a viewing angle that is not severely oblique. If you cannot get your eye (or a mirror or borescope) into position, that area is not inspected, and the record should say so.
- **The booth.** For fluorescent work: clean, uncluttered, and dark, with **ambient white light at or below about 2 footcandles (≈ 20 lux)**. Stray white light washes out fluorescent contrast.
- **UV-A intensity.** At least **1,000 microwatts per square centimeter at the examination surface**, verified with a calibrated meter after lamp warm-up, each shift, and after bulb or filter changes.
- **Glare and reflections.** Polished surfaces bounce UV into your eyes and create ghost glows — shift the lamp or the part, and keep filters clean and uncracked.
- **Visible particles.** The requirement flips: at least **100 footcandles (≈ 1,000 lux)** of white light at the surface, measured, not estimated.

These are typical procedure values consistent with practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7 — your written procedure governs.

sections.title: Lighting numbers to know cold
sections.body:
- Fluorescent: UV-A **≥ 1,000 µW/cm²** at the surface; ambient white light **≤ 2 fc (≈ 20 lux)**; dark-adaptation pause before examining.
- Visible: white light **≥ 100 fc (≈ 1,000 lux)** at the surface.
- All readings come from **calibrated meters at the examination surface** and go on the record. Typical values — the written procedure governs.

sections.heading: Component Condition and the Purpose of the Test
sections.body:
Interpretation happens in context, and two questions set that context.

**What has this part lived through?** A new forging is a candidate for seams, laps, and bursts; a welded fabrication for toe cracks and lack of fusion; an overhauled crankshaft for fatigue cracks at fillets, keyways, and oil holes — service-induced cracking concentrates where stress concentrates. Surface condition matters just as much: scale, rough machining, and thick paint hold particles mechanically and breed false indications, while any nonconductive coating left in place reduces sensitivity and must be within the limits your procedure allows.

**Why are we testing?** An in-process test between forging and machining hunts material flaws that machining will expose; final acceptance applies the contract criteria to the finished surface; an overhaul examination targets service damage in known hot spots. The purpose changes where you concentrate, which technique the procedure specifies, and which acceptance criteria apply — a flaw acceptable in a rough forging stage may be rejectable on a finished journal. Read the job paperwork before the first shot, not after the first indication.

sections.caption: Indication triage: prove an indication repeats, rule out geometry and magnetization artifacts, then characterize and evaluate only what remains.

sections.heading: Relevant, Nonrelevant, and False — Then Surface or Subsurface
sections.body:
**False indications** have no magnetic cause. Typical sources: particle drainage patterns, accumulations in rough surface texture or threads, lint and scale, and the general 'furring' of an over-magnetized part. Test of truth: clean the surface and retest under controlled conditions — a false indication fails to reproduce in place, or its cause is visibly mechanical. Furring across the whole surface says **reduce the amperage**.

**Nonrelevant indications** are real leakage from features that are not discontinuities:

- abrupt **section changes**, keyways, splines, and drilled holes near the surface
- **press-fit or shrink-fit boundaries** between mating parts
- the **permeability boundary** between weld metal and base metal
- **magnetic writing** — a touch from another magnetized part leaves fuzzy, loosely held scribbles; demagnetize and retest and they vanish
- **external poles** where flux enters and leaves (prod tips, yoke poles, coil ends)

Prove nonrelevance by correlating with the drawing and the magnetizing setup — never by assumption — and note it in the record.

**Whatever survives is relevant.** Then read depth from shape: **sharp, dense, tightly held** lines are surface-breaking cracks and seams; **broad, fuzzy, weakly held** patterns are subsurface. Depth judgment drives evaluation, because many criteria treat surface and subsurface indications differently.

sections.caption: Why appearance reveals depth: a surface-breaking crack concentrates leakage into a sharp, dense indication, while a subsurface discontinuity spreads weak leakage into a broad, fuzzy one.

sections.heading: Evaluation, Disposition, and Closing Out the Job
sections.body:
**Evaluation** measures a relevant indication against criteria that existed before you tested — from the design code, specification, or customer order. Typical criteria classify indications as **linear** (length ≥ 3 × width by common convention) or **rounded**, set maximum lengths by class or zone, limit aligned groups, and frequently allow **no relevant linear indications at all** in critical regions. Apply them exactly: measure, compare, decide. **Accept** what passes. **Reject** what fails — mark its location, quarantine or tag the part, and route it for disposition. Repaired areas return for reexamination with the same technique before acceptance, and where removal by grinding is authorized, the area is retested to prove the indication is gone and limits are still met.

**Postcleaning and protection (6.9).** Remove particle residue — in bearings, hydraulic passages, or under paint it causes real damage — and demagnetize and verify where required. Bare steel after a water-based bath flash-rusts: dry the part and apply the specified inhibitor or oil.

**Documentation (6.10).** Record part identity, procedure and revision, equipment, current type and amperage, technique and coverage, particle type and batch, light-meter readings, indications found (sketch, photo, or tape transfer with location and size), the disposition, and your name, certification level, and date. Retain per the quality system. If it is not written down, it did not happen.

interactive.title: Disposition the Indication
interactive.intro: An in-service pump shaft arrives for wet fluorescent MT on the bench unit. The work order cites ten years of service and a history of fatigue failures in this shaft family at the fillet radius. Walk the examination from first glow to final report.

steps.prompt: Under UV you see a sharp particle line exactly at the keyway corner. Your first move?
options: Reject the shaft — a sharp line is always a crack
Wipe the area, retest, and correlate the indication's position with the keyway geometry on the drawing before calling it
Ignore it — keyway indications never matter
steps.feedback: Abrupt geometry like a keyway corner produces real leakage that is nonrelevant — but you prove it, by reproducing the indication and matching it to the drawing, not by assuming. A crack at a keyway corner is also a classic fatigue site, so the correlation step is not optional.

steps.prompt: Mid-span you find a broad, fuzzy, weakly held band that smears when lightly wiped but reproduces in the same place. Best interpretation?
options: A tight surface fatigue crack
A subsurface discontinuity — leakage reaching the surface is weak and spread out
A drainage pattern from excess bath
steps.feedback: It reproduces in place, so it is not false. Broad and fuzzy is the signature of a subsurface source: the leakage field spreads before it reaches the surface, holding particles loosely over a wide footprint.

steps.prompt: At the fillet radius you find a sharp, dense, tightly held line running transverse to the shaft axis. Most likely call?
options: Magnetic writing from a magnetized hand tool
A relevant indication — a service-induced fatigue crack at a stress concentration
Furring from over-magnetization
steps.feedback: Sharp, dense, tightly held says surface-breaking crack; a fillet on a ten-year shaft with a known fatigue history says service-induced. Magnetic writing is fuzzy and vanishes after demag and retest; furring covers broad areas, not one tight line.

steps.prompt: The fillet indication measures longer than the acceptance criteria allow. What now?
options: Grind it out now and retest — keep the job moving
Reject: mark the location, document size and position, tag the shaft, and route it for disposition per the procedure
Downgrade it to nonrelevant since the customer needs the pump back
steps.feedback: Evaluation ends at the criteria. Rework such as grinding happens only when authorized by the disposition process, and the area is reexamined afterward. Schedule pressure never reclassifies an indication.

steps.prompt: The shaft is dispositioned and the examination is complete. What closes out the job?
options: Return the shaft as-is — the report is the repair shop's problem
Postclean particle residue, demagnetize and verify to the procedure limit, apply corrosion protection, and complete the report with settings, light readings, findings, and your signature and level
Just demagnetize — the bath protects the part from rust
steps.feedback: Closing out means postcleaning (residue damages bearings and hydraulics), verified demagnetization, corrosion protection after a water-based bath, and a complete record — part, procedure, settings, particle batch, lighting, findings, disposition, name, level, date.

checkQuestions.question: A sharp particle line appears at a press-fit collar boundary on a shaft. It reproduces after cleaning and retesting. The correct interpretation is most likely:
options: A relevant crack — anything that reproduces is a defect
A nonrelevant indication — real leakage at the fit boundary, confirmed by correlating with the assembly drawing
A false indication caused by bath drainage
Furring from excessive amperage
checkQuestions.explanation: A press-fit boundary produces genuine flux leakage without any discontinuity. It reproduces (so it is not false), but correlation with part geometry identifies it as nonrelevant — and that correlation must be done, then noted on the record.

checkQuestions.question: Why must ambient white light be held to about 2 footcandles in a fluorescent MT booth?
options: White light damages the UV lamp filter
Stray white light washes out the contrast between the fluorescing indication and the dark background, and it defeats dark adaptation
It keeps the bath from evaporating
It is only an energy-saving rule
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent inspection works because a small glowing indication stands out against a dark field; ambient white light raises the background and undoes the eye's dark adaptation, so typical procedures cap it near 2 fc (about 20 lux).

checkQuestions.question: Two indications reproduce on a part: one sharp, dense, and tightly held; the other broad, fuzzy, and weakly held. What does the difference tell you?
options: The sharp one is surface-breaking; the fuzzy one's source lies below the surface, because leakage spreads and weakens with depth
The sharp one is nonrelevant; the fuzzy one is relevant
Both must be subsurface since they reproduce
The fuzzy one is always lint contamination
checkQuestions.explanation: Leakage from a tight surface crack is concentrated and holds particles firmly in a sharp line; leakage from a subsurface discontinuity diffuses before reaching the surface, giving a broad, fuzzy, weakly held pattern.

checkQuestions.question: After a head shot, the entire surface of a part grows a uniform 'furry' coat of particles. The correct response is to:
options: Reject the part — total coverage means total cracking
Switch to dry powder
Recognize over-magnetization, reduce the amperage (or correct the technique), clean, and retest
Raise the amperage to burn through the fur
checkQuestions.explanation: Furring is a false-indication pattern caused by excessive magnetizing force pulling particles to the surface everywhere; the fix is a lower field — within procedure tolerances — then a clean retest.

checkQuestions.question: Why is postcleaning after MT a required step rather than a courtesy?
options: Leftover particles improve corrosion resistance and may be left in place
Residual particles in bearings, hydraulic passages, or under subsequent paint and plating cause damage and process failures — and water-based carriers left on bare steel invite rust
Postcleaning is only needed for dry powder
It only matters for fluorescent particles because they glow
checkQuestions.explanation: Particle residue is abrasive and mobile — it contaminates lubrication and hydraulic systems and ruins coating adhesion — and aqueous carriers flash-rust bare steel, so postcleaning plus corrosion protection closes every job.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §6.1–6.6 (human factors, continuity, viewing, lighting, component condition, purpose of test)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §6.7 (interpretation: relevant/nonrelevant, surface/subsurface)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §6.8–6.10 (evaluation, postcleaning and protection, documentation)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7 (lighting levels and examination records — general practice)
